goal_spec_json.goal_statement: Sleep a minimum of 6 hours consistently every night

full_morning_briefing.definition: Evaluate the morning_message together with the same-day generated_actions, because both are shown to the user as one morning briefing experience.
full_morning_briefing.morning_message:
Good morning, Ameena. You're reporting a 73 today with high energy — that's a solid start.

First, about last night: you got a whopping 9.38 hours of sleep, which is way above your usual and you fell asleep much earlier than you have been. That's a big shift. But your body's recovery signals tell a different story — your HRV is declining and below your baseline, which means even with all that time in bed, your system didn't get its usual deep recharge. This mismatch is key. Yesterday, you felt really low energy (24) and mood (45), and you also moved very little — only 950 steps with zero intense activity. Your nutrition was also light at 878 calories, which during Ramadan can definitely contribute to that drained feeling. So the picture is: a long, early night of sleep, but your body is still playing catch-up from a day of low fuel and low movement.

The good news is your stress was low yesterday (28), which is a real strength to build on. It means you have some mental space to work with today. Given all this, today is about honoring that high-energy feeling you woke up with, while gently supporting your body's actual need for recovery and stability after a taxing day.

Your actions today focus on creating small, intentional pauses to help stabilize your energy and protect your sleep rhythm.

You've got the awareness — today's about using those early bedtimes and low-stress moments as your foundation.

generated_actions.title: When you feel afternoon energy dip, do 2 minutes of focused breathing
generated_actions.rationale: Yesterday's subjective energy was critically low (24) despite a high overall score (73), and the subjective-objective gap is large (+21) and volatile. This implementation intention provides a friction-reduction tool to address the 'low energy score' soft constraint and 'mismatch_pattern' risk flag by grounding you in the present moment when fatigue hits.

evaluation.completion_prompt: Did you pause for a 2-minute breathing reset when you felt an energy dip this afternoon?
evaluation.success_definition: User reports intentionally taking a brief breathing break in response to an energy dip.

assumptions: You can take a brief mental break when needed
generated_actions.description: If you notice a drop in energy or focus this afternoon, stop for 2 minutes. Sit upright, close your eyes, and take 10 slow, deep breaths, focusing only on the sensation of air moving in and out.

generated_actions.title: Set a phone reminder for 10:30 PM to start winding down
generated_actions.rationale: Last night's sleep was exceptionally long with very early onset (43 min after midnight), a sharp reversal from recent late trends. However, your '6am basketball practices midweek' constraint conflicts with late sleep. This reminder anchors a consistent pre-bed routine to protect against schedule drift and supports today's 'sleep_schedule_consistency_with_recovery_focus' priority.

evaluation.completion_prompt: Did you set and honor the 10:30 PM wind-down reminder tonight?
evaluation.success_definition: User reports setting the reminder AND starting wind-down activities around that time, aiming for earlier sleep onset than recent extremes.

assumptions: You have a smartphone or device for reminders
generated_actions.description: Set a recurring alarm or calendar reminder for 10:30 PM tonight titled 'Wind Down Start'. When it goes off, begin transitioning to low-light, low-stimulus activities (no new work, dim lights).

generated_actions.title: Do a 3-minute gentle leg stretch right before getting into bed
generated_actions.rationale: Yesterday's activity was critically low (950 steps, 0 MVPA), which may contribute to physical stiffness despite long sleep. This low-effort movement promotes muscle relaxation and blood flow before bed, potentially improving sleep onset and efficiency, and addresses the 'low_recovery' risk flag by supporting physical recovery.

evaluation.completion_prompt: Did you do 3 minutes of gentle leg stretches immediately before bed?
evaluation.success_definition: User reports completing brief, gentle leg stretching as part of their pre-sleep routine.

assumptions: You can safely perform gentle leg stretches
generated_actions.description: When you are about to get into bed tonight, spend 3 minutes doing slow, gentle hamstring and calf stretches while standing or sitting on the edge of the bed.

generated_actions.title: After your first work block, take 60 seconds to look out a window
generated_actions.rationale: Given your 'tight schedule' and 'high workload' constraints, along with yesterday's low focus (57) and critically low energy (24), this tiny habit-stacked action provides a micro-break to reset visual and mental fatigue. It supports stability by introducing a deliberate pause, reducing cognitive spillover, and addressing the 'volatility' risk flag.

evaluation.completion_prompt: Did you take a 60-second window gaze break after your first work block?
evaluation.success_definition: User reports intentionally pausing after a work session to look into the distance for about a minute.

assumptions: You have access to a window or outdoor view
generated_actions.description: When you finish your first concentrated work session today, immediately get up and spend 60 seconds looking out a window at the farthest point you can see, letting your eyes relax.

must_avoid: looking at screens during this time

previous_day_generated_actions.title: When you start your first work task, take 30 seconds to check your posture
previous_day_generated_actions.rationale: Your subjective score (65) is lower than your physiological recovery suggests, continuing a mismatch pattern. This tiny, habit-stacked action promotes bodily awareness and grounding, which can help bridge the subjective-objective gap without cognitive effort, addressing the 'mismatch_pattern' and 'volatility' risk flags.

evaluation.completion_prompt: Did you pause to check and adjust your posture when starting your first work task?
evaluation.success_definition: User reports intentionally taking a brief posture check at the start of their work session.

assumptions: You will be working at a desk or seated
previous_day_generated_actions.description: As you begin your first work session today, pause for 30 seconds to consciously adjust your posture: sit upright, relax shoulders, and align your spine.

previous_day_generated_actions.title: After sunset, log your first meal with one protein description
previous_day_generated_actions.rationale: Yesterday's nutrition data was completely absent (meal_count_day=0, confidence=0), a persistent pattern likely related to Ramadan. This action directly tackles the 'nutrition_gap' and 'low_data_confidence' risk flags by making logging simple and protein-aware, encouraging both data quality and nutritional attention.

signal_refs.label: at least one meal logged today

evaluation.completion_prompt: Did you log your first meal after sunset and include a protein description?
evaluation.success_definition: At least one meal is logged today (ending the zero-meal streak) AND user reports including a protein source in the description.

assumptions: You are observing Ramadan and will break your fast at sunset
You have access to a nutrition logging app
previous_day_generated_actions.description: When you break your fast tonight, immediately log the meal in your app and include at least one protein source in the description (e.g., 'chicken', 'eggs', 'yogurt').

previous_day_generated_actions.title: Set a 'no new tasks' boundary 90 minutes before your target bedtime
previous_day_generated_actions.rationale: Your sleep schedule is very late and unstable (onset 34 min, wake 685 min, both trending later). Given your 'tight schedule' and 'high workload' constraints, this boundary protects your wind-down period from cognitive spillover, directly supporting your sleep goal and today's 'sleep_schedule_consistency_with_recovery_focus' priority.

evaluation.completion_prompt: Did you enforce a 'no new tasks' boundary 90 minutes before your intended bedtime?
evaluation.success_definition: User reports consciously avoiding starting new work or demanding tasks in the 90-minute pre-bed window.

assumptions: You have a target bedtime you can calculate
previous_day_generated_actions.description: When it's 90 minutes before your intended bedtime tonight, stop starting any new work or mentally demanding tasks. Switch to only finishing or winding down.

previous_day_generated_actions.title: Do 2 minutes of gentle neck and shoulder stretches right before bed
previous_day_generated_actions.rationale: Last night's sleep was long (9.48h) but had reduced REM and deep proportions, possibly indicating accumulated physical tension. This low-effort physical release leverages your excellent recovery (HRV 113.78) to promote relaxation at sleep onset, aiming to improve sleep architecture and efficiency.

evaluation.completion_prompt: Did you do 2 minutes of gentle neck/shoulder stretches right before getting into bed?
evaluation.success_definition: User reports completing brief, gentle stretching focused on neck and shoulders immediately before bed.

assumptions: You can move your neck and shoulders safely
previous_day_generated_actions.description: When you get into bed tonight, spend 2 minutes doing slow neck rolls and shoulder shrugs to release physical tension before lying down.

previous_day_briefing.morning_message:
Morning Ameena. You're feeling a 65 this morning — below your usual baseline, even though the numbers tell a different story. Let's unpack that.

First, sleep: you got a massive **9.5 hours last night** — that's over 3 hours more than your usual. The efficiency was solid at 87%, and your overnight recovery (HRV of 114) was actually strong. But here's the catch: your sleep architecture was off, with less REM and deep sleep than normal. Your body was in bed a long time, but it wasn't getting its usual quality of restorative cycles. This often happens when sleep is trying to compensate for something — maybe fatigue or stress we can't see because yesterday's mood and activity data are missing.

That brings us to the mismatch. You feel below average, but your physiology says you recovered well. This isn't the first time we've seen this gap. Without yesterday's subjective check-in, it's hard to know what weighed on you, but your body's long sleep suggests it was trying to balance *something*. Also, the nutrition data gap continues — no meals logged again yesterday, which fits your Ramadan rhythm but leaves your energy intake a mystery.

So today isn't about pushing harder. It's about **gentle grounding and protecting your wind-down**. Your actions are designed to bridge that feeling-versus-data gap with simple bodily awareness and to guard your evening so tonight's sleep can be both long *and* deep.

You've got strong recovery capacity — let's use it to align how you feel with how your body actually performed.

evidence_quotes: I am also very happy to be with my family
I am still hoping i can go back and finish the last couple of months of university in person
journal.goals_conflict_today: staying home vs returning to university

meal_descriptions.description: A spoon of fava beans, a scrambled egg, a slice or cheddar cheese and an Arabic bread
2 cheese sambosak and 2 chicken panne and 5 rolled cabbages stuffed with rice. A spoon of white rice and green beans
